$comment: SINGLE SOURCE OF TRUTH for the guest->host op-batch BINARY wire protocol (the reconciler flush path: guest emits a SerializedOperationBatch, host decodes it into DOM/UI mutations). Every codec MUST generate from or validate against THIS file, never hand-copy from a peer implementation. Authoritative implementations locked to it (M2): the Rust encoder crates/rill-guest/src/wire_encode.rs plus the two hardened streaming decoders src/host/wasm-guest/wire-decoder.ts (TS) and native/core/src/protocol/WireDecoder.cpp (C++), all conformance-tested against this schema and the golden/matrix vectors (op-batch-wire.golden.json, op-batch-wire.matrix.json). SUPERSEDED — pending reconciliation/retirement in M2: the old TS pair src/guest/runtime/reconciler/binary-encoder.ts (encoder) and src/shared/bridge/binary-protocol.ts (BinaryDecoder); they DIVERGE from this schema on FUNCTION (they still emit fnId-only + orphan intern entries for __name/__sourceFile, whereas this schema carries name/sourceFile/sourceLine on the wire via the FUNCTION flags byte). Do not treat the old pair as authoritative. A Rust/native codec generates its opcode + value-tag constants and its limit constants from this file the same way crates/rill-guest/build.rs generates from contracts/graphics-seams.json, and locks its record shapes to it via conformance tests + the golden vectors in op-batch-wire.golden.json. This is DELIBERATELY NOT FlatBuffers (see native/core/src/protocol/rill_protocol.fbs for the rejected eval): the whole point is the cross-batch string INTERN table, which FlatBuffers cannot express. DOM type names and prop names repeat ~80% across a batch, so interning is rill's key size win. Field names below are the exact record field order on the wire; opcode/tag numbers are the exact bytes. All integers are UNSIGNED unless the type says i32. Every multi-byte scalar is LITTLE-ENDIAN.

magic.$comment: First 4 bytes of every batch. The u32 value 0x4c4c4952 read little-endian spells the ASCII bytes 52 49 4C 4C = 'RILL'. A decoder MUST read a u32 LE and reject (fail-closed, decode nothing) if it is not exactly this value.

protocolVersion.$comment: u16 immediately after magic. A decoder MUST reject any value it does not implement. This file describes version 1 only.

typeAtoms.$comment: The scalar building blocks referenced by every 'type' field in the layouts below. All are little-endian. 'internRef' is the heart of the format: a u16 index into this batch's intern table (see 'intern'), used everywhere a string would otherwise be inlined.
typeAtoms.u64: 8 bytes unsigned little-endian. RESERVED and UNUSED in v1: no u64 field is emitted or decoded anywhere in a v1 batch (the HAS_TIMESTAMPS flag that would have used it is itself reserved + rejected, see batchFlags). Kept in this table only for forward-compat documentation.
typeAtoms.internRef: u16 index into the intern table; MUST be < the table's count. A decoder resolving an out-of-range index fails closed.
typeAtoms.utf8: raw UTF-8 code units, no terminator; always length-prefixed by the field that precedes it

header.$comment: Fixed 16-byte prefix. Written first-thing, fields in exactly this order. 'reserved' is three zero bytes rounding the header to 16.

layout.name: batchId
layout.note: monotonic batch sequence id chosen by the guest; opaque to the wire.

layout.name: opCount
layout.note: number of operation records that follow the intern table. Decoder reads exactly this many, never more (see limits.maxOps).

layout.name: flags
layout.note: bitmask, see batchFlags. Default emission is NONE (0x00). A decoder MUST reject fail-closed any batch that sets a RESERVED bit (DELTA_INTERN, STRUCTURAL_ONLY, HAS_TIMESTAMPS).

layout.name: reserved
layout.note: three padding bytes rounding the header to 16. The encoder MUST write them 0. A decoder MUST ignore them on read and MUST NOT reject a batch when they are nonzero — this is a forward-compat field, so a future version may repurpose them without breaking a v1 decoder.

batchFlags.$comment: Bit values OR'd into header.flags. NONE is what the reference encoder emits by default. DELTA_INTERN, STRUCTURAL_ONLY and HAS_TIMESTAMPS are ALL RESERVED bit positions in v1: the v1 encoder never sets any of them (it always emits the FULL intern table and never a per-op trailer), and a v1 decoder MUST reject fail-closed any batch whose flags set a reserved bit. In particular HAS_TIMESTAMPS is NOT decoded in v1 — it does not cause a u64 timestamp (or any other trailer) to be emitted or read after an operation record; no per-op timestamp exists in this version. These bits are defined only so a v1 decoder can recognise and reject a batch that uses a feature it does not implement.

intern.$comment: The cross-batch string dedup table, written immediately after the 16-byte header and BEFORE the operations. Every string on the wire (DOM type names, prop keys, text, string values, method/callId, error/regexp/promise fields, object keys) is emitted ONCE here and referenced elsewhere by a u16 internRef. Indices are 0-based and assigned in FIRST-APPEARANCE order during a pre-pass that walks the operations in array order (CREATE: type, then each prop key then its string value; UPDATE: each prop key then value, then removedProps; TEXT: text; REF_CALL: method, callId, then args). CROSS-BATCH SEMANTICS: a persistent encoder keeps the map alive across batches so a given string keeps a STABLE index for the whole session (indices grow monotonically as new strings appear); however in v1 the FULL current table is re-serialised in each batch (DELTA_INTERN is not emitted), so every batch is independently self-decodable and a decoder MUST rebuild its table fresh from each batch's 'entries'.

layout.name: count
layout.note: number of string entries; also the exclusive upper bound for every internRef in this batch. See limits.maxInternStrings.

record.name: byteLen
record.note: UTF-8 byte length of this string; see limits.maxStringBytes (a length that would not fit u16 MUST be rejected, never wrapped).

record.name: bytes
record.note: the raw UTF-8 bytes, no terminator.

opcodes.$comment: u8 tag that leads every operation record. Flat map for codegen (name -> byte).

propsTable.$comment: Reusable sub-structure used by CREATE and UPDATE for a SerializedValueObject of props. A u16 entry count followed by that many (key internRef, value) pairs. The value is encoded per the 'values' section.

layout.name: entryCount
layout.note: number of prop key/value pairs; u16-bounded (limits.maxCollectionElements).

ops.$comment: Fixed-order record layout for EVERY operation kind. Each record begins with its u8 opcode, then a u32 'id' (the target node id) for ALL kinds, then kind-specific fields in the order listed. v1 emits NO per-op trailer: the HAS_TIMESTAMPS flag that would have appended a u64 timestamp after each record is reserved and rejected in v1 (see batchFlags), so every record ends exactly at its last listed field. An unknown opcode is a fail-closed decode error for the whole batch. STRICT TRAILING (whole-batch decode contract): a decoder reads EXACTLY opCount operation records and MUST then have fully consumed the buffer — ZERO bytes may remain after the last record. Any trailing bytes are a fail-closed whole-batch reject (a batch is exactly its declared ops); the encoder writes nothing after the final record.

CREATE.description: Create a node of a given host component type with an initial prop set.

UPDATE.description: Patch props on an existing node: a set of props to add/change plus a list of prop names to remove.

APPEND.description: Append childId as the last child of parentId. ('id' carries the same node context as the other ops but is not used by the host for APPEND; write the operation's id.)

INSERT.description: Insert childId into parentId at a positional index.

REMOVE.description: Detach childId from parentId (without destroying it; DELETE destroys).

REORDER.description: Reorder parentId's children to exactly the given id sequence.

TEXT.description: Set the text content of a text node.

REF_CALL.description: Imperative ref method call from guest to a host node (e.g. focus/blur/measure/scrollTo), correlated by callId for the async result. NOTE: the SerializedRefCallOperation also carries refId, but refId is NOT on the wire; the decoder sets refId = id (the u32 above). args are encoded per the 'values' section.

layout.name: id
layout.note: the ref target node id; decoder mirrors this into both id and refId.

valueTags.$comment: u8 tag that leads every encoded SerializedValue. Flat map for codegen (name -> byte).

values.$comment: Every SerializedValue variant. Each value is a u8 tag followed by the payload below. NUMBER RULE: a JS number that Number.isInteger and fits [-2147483648, 2147483647] is encoded as INT32; every other finite number (fractional, or out of i32 range) is FLOAT64. NaN/Infinity are not legal here. An unknown tag is a fail-closed decode error. NOTE on FUNCTION: beyond __fnId, a function value carries the DevTools debug metadata the JS host consumes via receiver.ts/type-rules.ts — __name, __sourceFile, __sourceLine — each OPTIONAL and gated by a flags byte (see values.FUNCTION for the exact layout). Present strings are interned in first-appearance order AFTER fnId; sourceLine is an inline u32 (not interned). flags=0 means no metadata is present (one extra byte over a bare fnId).

BOOL_FALSE.note: the boolean false is the whole value (no payload byte).

BOOL_TRUE.note: the boolean true is the whole value (no payload byte).

FUNCTION.$comment: A function reference plus its OPTIONAL DevTools debug metadata (name/sourceFile/sourceLine) that the JS host reads via receiver.ts/type-rules.ts. Byte-exact layout: fnId (internRef u16), then a u8 'flags' byte, then ONLY the present optional fields IN THIS ORDER — name (internRef u16, if flags bit0), sourceFile (internRef u16, if flags bit1), sourceLine (u32, if flags bit2). Absent metadata => flags=0 and no trailing fields, i.e. exactly one extra byte over a bare fnId. The present strings are interned like every other string (first-appearance order, after fnId); sourceLine is an inline little-endian u32 (a 1-based line number), NOT interned.

payload.name: flags
payload.note: bit0=has_name, bit1=has_sourceFile, bit2=has_sourceLine; the other bits are reserved and MUST be written 0.

payload.name: name
payload.note: function name for DevTools; the whole field is absent (no bytes) when bit0 is clear.

payload.name: sourceFile
payload.note: source file path/URL for DevTools; absent (no bytes) when bit1 is clear.

payload.name: sourceLine
payload.note: 1-based source line number, inline u32 (not interned); absent (no bytes) when bit2 is clear.

payload.name: epochMs
payload.note: milliseconds since Unix epoch (from Date.getTime()); decoder rebuilds the ISO string.

payload.name: stack
payload.note: empty-string intern when stack is absent.

limits.$comment: FAIL-CLOSED caps. 'Fail-closed' means: on any breach the codec MUST abort the whole batch and let NOTHING partial cross the seam — an encoder throws before/without emitting a truncated buffer; a decoder throws and discards the whole batch rather than yielding a partial op list. These are hard structural bounds of the format (mostly u16 field widths), not tunable budgets. A native codec SHOULD generate these as constants (as crates/rill-guest/build.rs does for the graphics budget).

maxOps.reject: Encoder MUST NOT start a batch with more ops than this; decoder reads exactly opCount records and treats any opcode/underrun error as a whole-batch reject.

maxInternStrings.reason: intern.count is u16 and every internRef is u16, so indices span 0..65534.
maxInternStrings.reject: On interning the 65536th distinct string the encoder throws 'Intern table overflow' and emits nothing (the reference throws at internTable.length >= 65535). A decoder rejects any internRef >= intern.count.

maxStringBytes.reason: each intern entry's byteLen is u16.
maxStringBytes.reject: A string whose UTF-8 length exceeds this MUST be rejected (whole-batch, fail-closed) — the u16 length MUST NOT be allowed to wrap/truncate.

maxCollectionElements.reason: every count/length field (propsTable.entryCount, UPDATE.removedCount, REORDER.childCount, REF_CALL.argCount, OBJECT.entryCount, ARRAY.length, MAP.entryCount, SET.count) is u16.
maxCollectionElements.reject: A collection larger than this cannot be represented and MUST be rejected fail-closed rather than truncated.

maxBatchBytes.reason: reference encoder's default maxBufferSize (16 MiB) — the hard ceiling on one encoded batch buffer.
maxBatchBytes.reject: If the growing output would exceed this the encoder throws 'Buffer overflow' and emits nothing.

maxValueDepth.reason: bounds the recursion/allocation of nested container values (ARRAY/SET/MAP/OBJECT, and any value nested inside them) so a maliciously deep value tree cannot overflow a native decoder's C++ stack or blow up the TS decoder's memory. Depth counts nesting of container values only, starting at 1 for a top-level value; 64 is generous for real RN prop/style trees (which nest only a few levels).
maxValueDepth.reject: A codec MUST reject fail-closed (whole batch) before decoding/encoding a container value whose nesting would exceed this depth — the encoder throws before emitting and the decoder throws and discards the whole batch rather than recursing further.

maxTotalElements.definition: The maximum total number of DECODED ELEMENTS a single batch may contain. An element is: every VALUE NODE (each scalar value AND each container value OBJECT/ARRAY/MAP/SET, counted at every nesting level — a container's children are counted in addition to the container itself), PLUS every REORDER op childId, PLUS every UPDATE op removedProp reference (removedProps/removed internRef), PLUS every intern-table entry. Each of these counts as exactly 1. A codec MUST maintain ONE per-batch running count over all element kinds together and reject fail-closed (abort the whole batch) the moment that single count would exceed this cap, BEFORE allocating the offending element.
maxTotalElements.reason: Without this, a small (<= maxBatchBytes, i.e. <= 16 MiB) buffer inflates into multi-GB of decoded structures on the untrusted guest->host seam. Charging only value nodes left three non-value collections uncharged — REORDER childIds (u32 each), UPDATE removedProps/removed (internRef each), and the intern-table entries — each op is only u16-capped (65535) but the aggregate across maxOps ops was bounded solely by maxBatchBytes, so a legal <= 16 MiB batch packed with them decoded to ~320-395 MB (measured). Folding all element kinds into the one cap bounds ALL decoder allocation to this single limit (~210-220 MB C++ worst case: cap x the heaviest element, an OBJECT entry at ~200 B, plus the u16-capped ops vector and the <=16 MiB input buffer), closing the last non-value footprint path. 1048576 stays ~100x a realistic op-batch (whose intern + childIds + removedProps + values sum to a few thousand). maxBatchBytes remains the outer byte bound; this is the inner element-count bound.
maxTotalElements.reject: A codec MUST maintain ONE running total-element count across the whole batch — spanning every value node, every REORDER childId, every UPDATE removedProp reference, and every intern-table entry — and reject fail-closed (whole batch) the moment that count would exceed this cap, BEFORE allocating the offending element — the encoder throws before emitting and the decoder throws and discards the whole batch rather than materialising the next element.

maxDateMs.reason: A DATE value's epochMs must be finite and within the ECMAScript Date range (+/-8.64e15 ms). Beyond it a JavaScript Date is 'Invalid Date' (toISOString throws), so such a value could not survive a round-trip on the TS host.
maxDateMs.reject: The encoder MUST reject (throw, emit nothing) and every decoder MUST reject fail-closed (whole batch) a DATE whose epochMs is NaN, +/-Infinity, or whose absolute value exceeds this. All three codecs share this exact domain so the same bytes are valid everywhere (no drift).
